ThinkPHP CLI Commands and Database Migrations (Day 29)
PHP
MySQL
ThinkPHP 5.1
Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch
11th iThome Ironman Contest

Next up we'll get to know some of ThinkPHP's commonly used basic CLI commands. But the main focus of this post is migrations, because they'll affect how we work in the later chapters. We'll walk through the everyday commands, install think-migration, create migration files for our tables, write up() and down(), and finally seed the database with default data.

RE: Learning JS From Zero — Day 15: Vanilla AJAX (XMLHttpRequest)
JavaScript
RE：從零開始的學習 JS 生活
第 11 屆 iT邦幫忙鐵人賽

Day 15 of my reincarnated JS life. This time we dig into AJAX — what it actually is, why it changed the web, and how the native API XMLHttpRequest works under the hood. We'll also peek at the modern Fetch API and the old jQuery AJAX style so you can compare them side by side.
/en/javascript/2019/09/29/js-from-zero-14-json/
JS From Zero, Day 14: What Actually Is JSON (and Why It's Not JavaScript)

Day 14 of my JS-from-zero reincarnation, and this time we're tackling JSON. It looks a lot like JavaScript, but it's actually its own thing. We'll cover where it came from, why it beat out XML, and the two methods JavaScript gives you to convert between the two — JSON.stringify() and JSON.parse().
/en/php/2019/09/29/think-php-day-28-build-a-message-board/
Day 28 - Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch: Building a Message Board

We've covered most of the ThinkPHP basics by now, so this post walks through building a simple message board. I won't go deep into the code though — the focus here is on planning the message board, since it's basically just combining the techniques from earlier posts. I'll drop the GitHub repo at the end as a reference.
/en/php/2019/09/28/thinkphp-day-27-pagination/
ThinkPHP Pagination: Built-in Pages and a Custom Bootstrap Style
2019 iThome Ironman Contest

Pagination is one of those features pretty much every site needs, and the good news is ThinkPHP ships with it out of the box. In this post we'll call paginate(), wire it up with some HTML, and get a working pager. Then we'll go a step further and override the default Bootstrap style with our own. Finally, I'll show you the cleaner way to load that custom paginator from the extend folder instead of hacking the core.
/en/javascript/2019/09/28/js-from-zero-ep-13-dom/
JS From Zero EP.13: What Is the DOM, Really?

Day thirteen of my JavaScript journey. This post is all about the DOM — the Document Object Model. We'll cover what the DOM tree is, what DOM elements, text, and attributes are, and why a NodeList isn't quite an array. It's the foundational concept every web dev needs to get.
/en/php/2019/09/27/think-php-day-26-captcha/
Day 26 - Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch: Adding a Captcha
11th iT Ironman Contest

This post walks through ThinkPHP's built-in captcha feature. ThinkPHP ships captcha support out of the box, and unless you have some special requirement, the built-in one is more than enough. We'll add it to a form, wire up the validation error messages, and then build click-to-refresh and auto-refresh-every-60-seconds on top of it.
/en/javascript/2019/09/27/js-from-zero-ep12-loops/
JS From Zero EP.12 — Loops: for, while, and do...while

Day twelve of being reborn into JavaScript, and this time we're talking about loops. We'll go through the three basic ones — for, while, and do...while — how they actually run, and the classic beginner trap of writing an infinite loop by forgetting to update the counter.
/en/php/2019/09/26/thinkphp-day-25-form-token/
Stopping CSRF in ThinkPHP with Form Tokens

Now that form validation is out of the way, it's time to talk about form Tokens. A form Token only works alongside the validation we set up earlier, which is exactly why I saved it for after the validation chapter. Let's dig into what a form Token actually is and how it stops CSRF attacks in ThinkPHP.
/en/javascript/2019/09/26/js-from-zero-ep-11-type-coercion/
RE: Learning JS From Zero — Day 11: Why Does 1 == '1' Return true?

Day 11 of the reincarnation. This time we dig into type coercion in JavaScript — one of the most common and most important concepts in the language. We'll talk about weak vs strong typing, why JavaScript loves to convert types behind your back, and why == can return some genuinely weird results.
